commit.message:
[TST](wal3): replace sleep-based waits with polling in read repair tests (#6624)

## Description of changes

Replace fixed-duration tokio::time::sleep calls with deadline-based
polling loops in fragment_manager read repair tests.  The old approach
was flaky under heavy test parallelism because a fixed sleep (100-500ms)
could expire before the async repair task completed.

Each polling loop now:
- Checks the actual condition (object exists, permit released, etc.)
- Uses a generous 30-second deadline with 50ms polling interval
- Asserts with a descriptive message on timeout

Also relax the read-does-not-block-on-repair duration threshold from
50ms to 5s to account for slow S3 GETs under load while still catching
the case where a read accidentally awaits the repair PUT.

## Test plan

CI

## Migration plan

N/A

## Observability plan

N/A

## Documentation Changes

N/A

commit.message:
[ENH](wal3): handle AlreadyExists alongside Precondition errors (#6625)

## Description of changes

Some storage backends return StorageError::AlreadyExists instead of
StorageError::Precondition when a conditional put (IfNotExist) fails.
Handle both error variants at every contention point:

- batch_manager: treat AlreadyExists as LogContentionFailure
- manifest_manager initialize: return AlreadyInitialized error
- manifest_manager put: treat AlreadyExists as LogContentionDurable
- manifest_manager publish: treat AlreadyExists as contention

commit.message:
[ENH]Add ClientFactory for CompactorClient (#6626)

Adds a ClientFactory implementation for the compactor gRPC client so
compaction nodes discovered via the memberlist can be used with
ClientAssigner and ClientManager.

Changes:

* Implement ClientFactory for
  `CompactorClient<GrpcClientTraceService<Channel>>` in
  `rust/memberlist/src/client_manager.rs`, matching the existing pattern
  used for `LogServiceClient`, `QueryExecutorClient`, and
  `HeapTenderServiceClient`.
* `new_from_channel` wraps the channel with
  `CompactorClient::new(channel)`; `max_decoding_message_size` delegates
  to the tonic client’s builder.

Now, external callers (e.g. ops service) can construct
`ClientAssigner<CompactorClient<...>>` and use it with `ClientManager`
and a memberlist provider (e.g. `CustomResourceMemberlistProvider`) to
talk to compaction-service nodes by node name or assignment key (e.g.
collection ID), in the same way as the log service client.

commit.message:
[BUG] Fix sysdb OTEL metrics service name (#6565)

## Description of changes

Currently sysdb metrics are not associated with any otel service. This
PR fixes the OTEL initialization so that the service name is specified
for the OTEL metrics. Additionally, it updates our default chart
overrides so that sysdb sends both traces and metrics to otel-collector.
That way otel-collector will route traces to jaeger and it will route
metrics to prometheus.

[ENH] 1 bit rabitq quantization implementation (#6570)

## Description of changes

Add Code<1> alongside the existing Code<4>, refactoring the quantization
module into separate single_bit/multi_bit files with shared distance
math in utils. The 1-bit path uses sign-bit quantization with
SIMD-accelerated distance kernels: float-based signed_dot, bitwise
AND+popcount via QuantizedQuery, and nibble LUT via BatchQueryLuts.

commit.message:
[ENH]: S3 client config improvements (#6618)

## Description of changes

_Summarize the changes made by this PR._

- Improvements & Bug fixes
- Set read_timeout_ms in s3 client. This is the timeout for Time For
First Byte (TFFB). Currently, it can happen that the client is stuck for
60 secs without getting even the headers. Better to timeout and retry.
Also, note that Stall protection does not kick in until then
  - The client sdk transparently retries in this case
- Also lower the default stall_protection_ms to 5 secs. 15 secs felt on
the higher side
- New functionality

commit.message:
[ENH] Warn on no API key set on JS EFs (#6599)

On JS EFs, we want to log a warning when the required API key is not set
instead of throwing an exception. This allows the client to build the
schema from the server instead of defaulting to ambiguous schema state.

commit.message:
[ENH] Use buffer_unordered for lazy fragment fetch concurrency (#6584)

## Description of changes

Replace try_join_all + semaphore with futures::stream::buffer_unordered
in FragmentFetcher::fetch_records.  The previous approach eagerly
constructed all futures upfront, holding resources for every fragment
even while waiting on the semaphore.  buffer_unordered constructs
futures lazily so that at most max_concurrency are alive at once,
reducing peak memory usage and task overhead for large fragment lists.

- Change fetch_records receiver from &self to self: &Arc<Self> so the
  fetcher can be cloned into each stream future
- Add explicit drop(bytes) after parquet parsing to release the buffer
  before filtering records
- Update tests to wrap FragmentFetcher in Arc

## Test plan

CI

## Migration plan

N/A

## Observability plan

I have a metric that goes up when I send load. I expect it to do so to a
lesser degree.

## Documentation Changes

N/A

Co-authored-by: AI

commit.message:
[ENH](system) add OpenTelemetry metrics to system crate (#6537)

## Description of changes

Instrument the execution pipeline with comprehensive metrics covering
queue depths, task lifecycle, latency, and error rates.  Key additions:

Dispatcher: task/waiter/IO-slot depth histograms, enqueue/dispatch/abort
counters, queue latency, worker send failures, and a static HOSTNAME
cache via LazyLock.  Expose DispatcherStats for runtime inspection.

Operator/Task: per-task started/completed/reply-fail counters and
queue/run/end-to-end latency histograms with operator and task_type
attributes.  Extract handle_reply_failure and record_task_completion
helpers to deduplicate error-reporting logic.

Orchestrator: run/terminate counters, run and cleanup latency
histograms,
and initial-task counting, all keyed by orchestrator name and result.

Worker: request, task-run, and dispatcher-send-fail counters with
per-worker-id attribution and task run latency.

Executor: message-received, handler-completed, stop, and on_stop latency
metrics.  Return MessageExecutionOutcome from wrapped message handlers
to distinguish ok vs panic. Track ComponentRuntimeStats (handled message
count and last error) and expose ComponentStats on ComponentHandle.

Scheduler: scheduled/executed/cancelled/send-fail counters and delay
histograms keyed by component and kind (once vs interval).

Channel: send/request counters and request latency on ComponentSender,
with failure-reason attributes (full, closed, send, recv).

Also add duration_ms utility in utils and derive Copy on OperatorType.

## Test plan

CI

## Migration plan

N/A

## Observability plan

The PR is the plan.

## Documentation Changes

N/A

Co-authored-by: AI

commit.message:
[ENH](log) add pointer-based log fetch via ScoutLogFragments (#6533)

## Description of changes

Introduce a new ScoutLogFragments RPC that returns fragment pointers so
query and compactor nodes can read log data directly from object storage
instead of streaming through gRPC PullLogs.

Key changes:
- Add ScoutLogFragments RPC and LogFragmentPointer message to proto
- Implement server-side handler in log-service using wal3 scan
- Add scout_log_fragments client method to grpc_log and Log enum
- Create FragmentFetcher in rust/worker that dereferences pointers,
  reads parquet fragments from storage, and caches raw bytes
- Extend FetchLogOperator with use_pointer_fetch toggle to select
  between gRPC pull and pointer-based fetch strategies
- Thread use_pointer_fetch config through compactor and query service
- Default use_pointer_fetch to false for safe incremental rollout

## Test plan

CI

N/A

Staging

commit.message:
[ENH] use ResourceExhausted error code for log backpressure (#6527)

## Description of changes

Change GrpcPushLogsError::Backoff and BackoffCompaction to return
ResourceExhausted instead of AlreadyExists, which more accurately
represents the backpressure semantics.

Update frontend retry logic to retry on ResourceExhausted instead of
AlreadyExists, and skip retries when the backpressure is caused by
compaction lag.  This prevents the frontend from pointlessly retrying
operations that will not succeed until compaction catches up.

commit.message:
[BUG] CAS on version during reassignment (#6529)

## Description of changes

_Summarize the changes made by this PR._

- Improvements & Bug fixes
- In the existing code, when `remove` and `reassign` are concurrent, it
is possible to have the following scenario:
    - `reassign` start, pass version check
    - `remove` increment version to invalidate entry
- `reassign` increment version and register point in clusters, restoring
the delete
- The patch is to perform CAS on version increment during `reassign`, in
the same way as the full precision SPANN implementation.
- New functionality
  - N/A

commit.message:
[BUG](storage): retry batch fetch on channel closure (#6526)

## Description of changes

Wrap the dedup-aware batch fetch path in a retry loop (up to 2
attempts). When the oneshot channel receiver encounters a closure
error—indicating the driving task was dropped before sending its
result—the request is retried immediately instead of propagating
the error. Inner operation errors still fail fast.

This addresses transient failures where a spawned fetch task gets
dropped (e.g. due to cancellation) before delivering results to
dedup-waiting callers.

tests.

commit.message:
[PERF] Parallelize segment reader initialization in filter and idf operators (#6522)

## Description of changes

- Improvements & Bug fixes
- Refactored `Filter` and `Idf` operators to initialize
`RecordSegmentReader` and `MetadataSegmentReader` in parallel using
`tokio::join!` instead of sequentially awaiting them
- This optimization reduces latency by eliminating unnecessary
sequential waiting on independent async operations
- Both readers are now created concurrently since they have no
dependencies on each other

## Test plan

- Existing tests pass (no new test cases needed as this is a refactor of
async execution order)
- Changes are localized to operator initialization logic and maintain
the same error handling semantics

## Migration plan

N/A - This is a performance optimization with no API or data model
changes.

## Observability plan

The existing tracing spans for segment reader creation are preserved,
allowing monitoring of initialization performance improvements.

## Documentation Changes

N/A - No user-facing API changes.

commit.message:
[BUG]: Drop pydantic v1 compatibility layer for Python 3.14 support (#6356)

Fixes #5996 — chromadb crashes on import with Python 3.14 because
`pydantic.v1` compat layer breaks on 3.14.

The rest of the codebase already uses pydantic v2 APIs, so this just
drops the v1 shim in `config.py`:

- Use `pydantic_settings.BaseSettings` + `pydantic.field_validator`
directly
- Add missing type annotations on 3 fields (`chroma_coordinator_host`,
`chroma_logservice_host`, `chroma_logservice_port`) — required by
pydantic v2
- `class Config` → `model_config` dict
- Bump dep to `pydantic >= 2.0`, add `pydantic-settings >= 2.0`

There's a larger PR in #5842 that also cleans up `fastapi/__init__.py`
and `types.py`, but those files already work fine — this is just the
minimum to unbreak 3.14.

Tested on Python 3.14.1: import works, `test_config.py` passes,
`PersistentClient` round-trips documents.

---------

Co-authored-by: Kyle Diaz <kyle@trychroma.com>

commit.message:
[ENH](scheduler): batch sysdb queries in collection enrichment (#6496)

## Description of changes

Group collections by topology and fetch them in a single
get_collections call per group instead of one query per collection.
This reduces sysdb round-trips from O(N) to O(topologies).

Remove the get-last-compaction-time call; the information's not
used and the cost is too great.

Replace the panic on log-offset-precedes-sysdb-position with a
tracing::error so a single inconsistent collection does not crash
the compactor.

Derive Eq, PartialEq, and Hash on DatabaseOrTopology in both sysdb
crates so it can be used as a HashMap key for the grouping.

Refactor the monolithic scheduler test into a SchedulerFixture with
focused, serial test cases for each behavior (empty memberlist,
disabled collections, failure thresholds, etc.).

## Test plan

CI

## Migration plan

N/A

## Observability plan

We should see reduced calls to sysdb.

## Documentation Changes

N/A

Co-authored-by: AI

commit.message:
[ENH](log-service): separate S3 and repl dirty log roll-ups (#6465)

## Description of changes

Split the dirty log rolling mechanism into independent S3 and
replication
paths so they can run concurrently without blocking each other.
Previously
a slow topology roll-up would delay S3 compaction signals and vice
versa.

- Split `rolling_up` mutex into `rolling_up_s3` and `rolling_up_repl`
- Split `need_to_compact` into `need_to_compact_s3` (keyed by
  CollectionUuid) and `need_to_compact_repl` (keyed by
  (TopologyName, CollectionUuid))
- Replace monolithic `roll_dirty_log` with `roll_dirty_log_s3_cycle`
  and `roll_dirty_log_repl_cycle` running concurrently via tokio::join
- Extract `should_compact` and `likely_needs_purge_dirty` methods on
  RollupPerCollection to deduplicate compaction selection logic
- Merge backpressure and metrics from both maps after each cycle

## Test plan

chroma-log-service, wal3: local
the rest: CI

## Migration plan

N/A

## Observability plan

N/A

## Documentation Changes

N/A

Co-authored-by: AI

commit.message:
[ENH]: Allow rebuild to specify segments (#6478)

## Description of changes

This change makes it possible to invoke rebuild on specific segments of a collection instead of the whole collection. It also prevents rebuild from overwriting the record segment.

It operates by threading through a set of SegmentScope objects into the comapction API that controls what segments an Apply task is created for in the ApplyLogsOrchestrator.

Note: This still gets all the writers during compaction but that's a more involved code change to get rid of that too.

Sample usage:

```
# Rebuild only metadata segment
cargo run -- rebuild --id <collection-uuid> --segment metadata
 
# Rebuild only vector segment  
cargo run -- rebuild --id <collection-uuid> --segment vector
 
# Rebuild multiple specific segments
cargo run -- rebuild --id <collection-uuid> --segment metadata --segment vector
```

commit.message:
[ENH] Add close() method and context manager support to Client (#6373)

## Summary
Fixes #5868

Added `close()` method and context manager support to the `Client` class
to properly release resources and close SQLite connections. This
resolves file locking issues when using `PersistentClient` with
operations that require exclusive file access, such as uploading
database files to S3.

## Changes
- Added `Client.close()` method that calls `self._system.stop()` to
properly stop the system and release resources
- Implemented `__enter__` and `__exit__` methods for context manager
support
- Added comprehensive tests for both `close()` and context manager usage
with `PersistentClient` and `EphemeralClient`

## Test Plan
Added four new test cases in `chromadb/test/test_client.py`:
- [x] `test_persistent_client_close()` - Tests explicit close() call
with PersistentClient
- [x] `test_persistent_client_context_manager()` - Tests context manager
with PersistentClient
- [x] `test_ephemeral_client_close()` - Tests explicit close() call with
EphemeralClient
- [x] `test_ephemeral_client_context_manager()` - Tests context manager
with EphemeralClient

All tests verify that:
1. The system is running before close/context exit
2. The system is stopped after close/context exit
3. Data persists correctly (for PersistentClient)

## Usage Examples
```python
# Explicit close
client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db")
collection = client.create_collection("my_collection")
collection.add(ids=["id1"], documents=["doc1"])
client.close()

# Context manager (recommended)
with chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db") as client:
    collection = client.create_collection("my_collection")
    collection.add(ids=["id1"], documents=["doc1"])
# Automatically closed, safe to upload files to S3 now
```

## Related Issues
This addresses the core issue reported in #5868 where users experienced
SQLite file locking problems when trying to upload ChromaDB files to AWS
S3 from Lambda functions. The workaround mentioned in the issue was to
use `client._system.stop()`, which this PR now exposes as a public API
method.

```python
collection = client.create_collection("my_collection")
collection.add(ids=["id1"], documents=["doc1"])
client.close()

# Context manager (recommended)
with chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chroma_db") as client:
    collection = client.create_collection("my_collection")
    collection.add(ids=["id1"], documents=["doc1"])
# Automatically closed, safe to upload files to S3 now
```

## Related Issues
This addresses the core issue reported in #5868 where users experienced
SQLite file locking problems when trying to upload ChromaDB files to AWS
S3 from Lambda functions. The workaround mentioned in the issue was to
use `client._system.stop()`, which this PR now exposes as a public API
method.

commit.message:
[ENH](log-service): add separate concurrency limit for manifest loads (#6364)

## Description of changes

Add rollup_concurrent_manifests config to limit concurrent replicated
manifest operations during dirty log rollup, independent from the
general rollup_concurrency setting.

Changes:
- Add semaphore to limit concurrent manifest loads (default 50)
- Increase rollup_concurrency default from 50 to 500 for cursor loading
- Remove unnecessary tracing span from cursor load path
- Add documentation explaining the math behind default values

The new defaults allow processing 50k dirty collections in under 2s
while keeping manifest operations at 1/8 of Spanner node capacity.

commit.message:
[ENH]: Code impl for Block with more efficient estimated_size() function (#6448)

## Description of changes

foyer had a blanket Code implementation for types that were Serialize + Deserialize. Its estimated_size() function operates by serializing its input and then wastefully finding the size of that. This change explicitly implements Code for Block to avoid this inefficiency.

- Improvements & Bug fixes
  - Foyer insertions should be faster now. It calls estimated_size() on the admission control path while further up the callstack chroma blockstorage potentially holds a NAC permit.
- New functionality
  - N/A

## Test plan
Tests have been added to the affected file to ensure that the new Code trait is interoperable with Blocks serialized via the old trait.

- [ ] Tests pass locally with `pytest` for python, `yarn test` for js, `cargo test` for rust

commit.message:
[ENH] Truncate repr of test record set types for readable hypothesis output (#6454)

## Description of changes

Introduce `_TruncatedReprDict` base class that caps repr() at 1000
characters, preventing hypothesis from producing overwhelming error
messages when tests fail with large record sets.

RecordSet, NormalizedRecordSet, and StateMachineRecordSet now inherit
from `_TruncatedReprDict` instead of TypedDict, and all construction
sites are updated from dict literal syntax to constructor calls.

## Test plan

I expect to see CI not give pages of vectors when there's a flake.

readme_html:
pip install chromadb # python client
# for javascript, npm install chromadb!
# for client-server mode, chroma run --path /chroma_db_path

Chroma Cloud

Our hosted service, Chroma Cloud, powers serverless vector, hybrid, and full-text search. It's extremely fast, cost-effective, scalable and painless. Create a DB and try it out in under 30 seconds with $5 of free credits.

Get started with Chroma Cloud

API

The core API is only 4 functions (run our 💡 Google Colab):

import chromadb
# setup Chroma in-memory, for easy prototyping. Can add persistence easily!
client = chromadb.Client()

# Create collection. get_collection, get_or_create_collection, delete_collection also available!
collection = client.create_collection("all-my-documents")

# Add docs to the collection. Can also update and delete. Row-based API coming soon!
collection.add(
    documents=["This is document1", "This is document2"], # we handle tokenization, embedding, and indexing automatically. You can skip that and add your own embeddings as well
    metadatas=[{"source": "notion"}, {"source": "google-docs"}], # filter on these!
    ids=["doc1", "doc2"], # unique for each doc
)

# Query/search 2 most similar results. You can also .get by id
results = collection.query(
    query_texts=["This is a query document"],
    n_results=2,
    # where={"metadata_field": "is_equal_to_this"}, # optional filter
    # where_document={"$contains":"search_string"}  # optional filter
)

Learn about all features on our Docs

Features

  • Simple: Fully-typed, fully-tested, fully-documented == happiness
  • Integrations: 🦜️🔗 LangChain (python and js), 🦙 LlamaIndex and more soon
  • Dev, Test, Prod: the same API that runs in your python notebook, scales to your cluster
  • Feature-rich: Queries, filtering, regex and more
  • Free & Open Source: Apache 2.0 Licensed

Use case: ChatGPT for ______

For example, the "Chat your data" use case:

  1. Add documents to your database. You can pass in your own embeddings, embedding function, or let Chroma embed them for you.
  2. Query relevant documents with natural language.
  3. Compose documents into the context window of an LLM like GPT4 for additional summarization or analysis.

Embeddings?

What are embeddings?

  • Read the guide from OpenAI
  • Literal: Embedding something turns it from image/text/audio into a list of numbers. 🖼️ or 📄 => [1.2, 2.1, ....]. This process makes documents "understandable" to a machine learning model.
  • By analogy: An embedding represents the essence of a document. This enables documents and queries with the same essence to be "near" each other and therefore easy to find.
  • Technical: An embedding is the latent-space position of a document at a layer of a deep neural network. For models trained specifically to embed data, this is the last layer.
  • A small example: If you search your photos for "famous bridge in San Francisco". By embedding this query and comparing it to the embeddings of your photos and their metadata - it should return photos of the Golden Gate Bridge.

Chroma allows you to store these vectors or embeddings and search by nearest neighbors rather than by substrings like a traditional database. By default, Chroma uses Sentence Transformers to embed for you but you can also use OpenAI embeddings, Cohere (multilingual) embeddings, or your own.
